1:
David's last words open a passage the witnesses handle with unusual reverence, and they divide first over what "last" means. John Gill insists these are not the final syllables David ever spoke — he counselled Solomon and ordered the temple afterward — but the last he uttered "under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit, or that he delivered out by way of prophecy," fitly placed after he had finished the book of Psalms; the Targum, Gill notes, reads them as a prophecy of Messiah and of "the days of consolation that should come." Adam Clarke narrows it further to the last poetical composition, since David "might have spoken many words after these in prose, but none in verse" — a song holding "a glorious prediction of the Messiah's kingdom and conquests."

The grammatical-historical commentators fasten on the solemn form of the superscription. Keil and Delitzsch show that the whole introduction rests upon Balaam's oracles (Num. 24:3, 24:15), David deliberately expanding the prophecy of the Star out of Jacob; like Balaam he calls his utterance a ne'um, a divine saying or oracle. Charles Ellicott presses the same point from the doubled verb "said," a peculiar form used two or three hundred times of divine utterances and applied to a human speaker only here, in the Balaam passages, and in Proverbs 30:1 — everywhere a special claim to inspiration. The "sweet psalmist of Israel" he renders literally, "he that is pleasant in Israel's psalms."

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the verse as David's last will and testament, and dwells on its two poles: the meanness of his origin — "the son of Jesse," the rock out of which he was hewn — and the height of his elevation, raised up as prince (the anointed of the God of Jacob) and as prophet-psalmist. That David was a psalmist Henry reckons no lesser honour than that he was a king: "it is true preferment to be serviceable to the church" in acts of prayer and praise.

One genuine disagreement surfaces over who speaks the verse. John Wesley, eyeing the grammar, judges that "these seem not to be the words of David, but of the sacred penman of this book" — the historian describing the prophet — whereas Henry and Gill hear David himself rehearsing, in the third person, the grace that lifted him. All agree, however, on the burden Wesley states plainly: when death approaches "we should endeavour both to honour God, and to profit others with our last words," and those who have tasted "the pleasantness of the ways of wisdom" should leave their testimony behind. The shepherd-boy turned king and singer bows out claiming not his conquests but his inspiration.
2:
This verse is the ground on which all that follows stands, and the witnesses read it with one voice as David's own claim to inspiration — indeed, to a fully verbal inspiration. John Gill takes it first negatively: the psalms and songs "were not the fruits of his own genius, but were written by him under the inspiration of the Spirit of God"; David was one of those holy men who "spoke, even as they were moved by the Holy Ghost." He weighs the Hebrew that may be rendered "spake in me," and draws out the sequence — what David uttered "was first internally impressed upon his mind by the Spirit of God, and then he expressed it with his tongue."

The Wesleyan reading sharpens the point into a doctrine of the very words. Adam Clarke distinguishes the two clauses with precision: "The Spirit of the Lord spake by me — hence the matter of his writing came by direct and immediate inspiration. His word was in my tongue — hence the words of this writing were as directly inspired as the matter." Not the substance only but the language is God-given. John Wesley presses the same conviction across David's whole output: these words, "and consequently the other words and Psalms composed and uttered by me upon the like solemn occasions, are not to be looked upon as human inventions," for both matter and words "are suggested by God's Spirit, the great teacher of the church."

The grammatical-historical commentators confirm rather than complicate this. Charles Ellicott notes that, "in accordance with" the superscription of the previous verse, here and in the next clause there is "most explicit assertion that this was spoken under the prompting and guidance of the Divine Spirit." Keil and Delitzsch, reading on into the oracle, tie the utterance to its Balaam-shaped form and to the promise of 2 Samuel 7, so that the dying king's song is set within the current of earlier prophecy rather than beside it.

There is no debate here, only a united confession. What the Spirit worked in David's heart, the Spirit shaped upon David's tongue; the singer is at once the author and the instrument. The verse thus does for the song what the apostle would later do for all Scripture — it locates the words in the mouth of God before they were ever in the mouth of the man. On this footing the great sentence of the next verses, that a just ruler must reign "in the fear of God," comes forward not as royal opinion but as the word of "the God of Israel" and "the Rock of Israel."
3:
Now the oracle proper begins, and the witnesses concentrate on the twin titles of the Speaker and on the charter of just rule He lays down. John Gill hears in "the God of Israel" the covenant God, who is "the covenant God and Father of the whole spiritual Israel." John Wesley adds the force of the second title: the "Rock" is "the strength, and defence, and protector of his people," who shows Himself such precisely "by directing kings and rulers so to manage their power as may most conduce to their comfort and benefit."

On the maxim itself — He that ruleth over men must be just, ruling in the fear of God — the schools agree in substance while pulling gently in two directions. Charles Ellicott confesses the Hebrew "exceedingly elliptical," yet finds the sentence unsurpassable: "The fundamental point of all just government has never been more perfectly set forth — that it must be 'in the fear of God.'" Wesley reads the two clauses as "the two principal parts of a king's duty, answerable to the two tables of God's law, justice towards men, and piety towards God." So read, the verse is the ideal of every magistrate.

The Wesleyan Clarke, however, turns the same ellipsis toward a single coming King. Rendering the words "He that ruleth in man is the just one," or "the just one is the ruler among men," he takes them of Christ: "it is by God's fear that Jesus Christ rules the hearts of all his followers; and he who has not the fear of God before his eyes, can never be a Christian." The grammatical-historical Keil pushes furthest in this direction: the ruler David "sees in spirit" is not merely any righteous magistrate, for such a reading "overlooks Nathan's promise" of 2 Samuel 7 and "weakens the force of the saying so solemnly introduced as the word of God." The just ruler is the promised seed.

Here, then, is the chapter's first quiet division: is verse 3 a rule for kings in general (Wesley, Ellicott) or a prophecy of the one King in particular (Clarke, Keil)? The two need not exclude one another, and the witnesses do not force them to. Every throne is bound by this word, and every throne falls short of it — which is why the oracle presses on, past the ideal, to the covenant that alone secures a house "not so with God." What David's own sons could not embody, the description in v.4 holds out as pure promise: a reign like cloudless morning.
4:
The oracle now clothes the just ruler in three images of morning — sunrise, a cloudless sky, and grass quickened by rain — and the witnesses debate whether these describe the king's duty or his glory. John Wesley keeps the accent on duty: the verse is "a farther description of the king's duty," to rule "not only with justice and piety, but also with sweetness, and gentleness, and condescension to the infirmities of his people," so that his government is as welcome "as is the sun-shine in a clear morning, or the tender grass which springs out of the earth" after rain.

The other voices turn the images from obligation to blessing, and press them toward Christ. John Gill makes the ruler "the light and glory of his people," whose administrations "are pleasant and delightful, and promise a growing and increasing happiness"; and he heaps up the titles under which these metaphors "well suit" the Messiah — "the true light," "the morning star," "the day-spring from on high," "the sun of righteousness," and "the light of the world." Adam Clarke frankly calls the verse "very obscure," since it is not plain who is meant; taking the Messiah for the whole subject, he reports Kennicott's manuscript that supplies the name Jehovah and yields, "As the light of the morning ariseth Jehovah" — "the Sun of righteousness, bringing salvation in his rays."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott ties the picture firmly to Nathan's promise and to Psalm 72, where the same blessings "are depicted as flowing from such a government." He is candid about David's realism: the king "knew far too much of the evil of his own heart and of the troubles in his household to suppose that his ideal could be perfectly realised in any other of his descendants than in Him who should crush the serpent's head." Ellicott even re-punctuates the sentence so that it runs on into the next verse — "as the tender grass grows from the earth: is not my house so with God?" — making the morning-vision the very thing David's family so painfully lacked.

So the division is real but not sharp. Wesley alone hears a charge to reign gently; Gill, Clarke, and Ellicott hear a promise of the reign that will one day dawn without cloud. Both readings meet in the confession that no earthly house has kept such a morning — which is exactly why the oracle turns, in v.5, from the radiant ideal to the everlasting covenant that holds when the sunshine fails.
5:
This is the verse the witnesses find hardest, and their division is grammatical before it is theological. The whole difficulty lies in the little words "not so." Charles Ellicott lays out the fork: the rendering of the English, the LXX, the Vulgate and the Syriac makes David "recognise how far he and his house have failed to realise the ideal," yet rest on a promise that must be fulfilled in his posterity; "most modern commentators, however, prefer to take the clauses interrogatively — 'Is not my house thus with God? for He hath made with me an everlasting covenant.'" The Hebrew, he grants, "admits either rendering," though the ancient versions give "a higher idea of David's spiritual discernment." Keil and Delitzsch and Adam Clarke both take the interrogative road — Clarke would read kun, "established," for ken, "so," making the sense, "for is not my house established with God?"

Beneath the dispute over punctuation the witnesses are wholly united on the substance, which is the everlasting covenant. John Gill reads David's "although my house be not so" against the wreckage of his own family — "the cases of Amnon, Absalom, and Adonijah" — yet finds the kingdom sure "in the Messiah that should spring from him," secured by covenant; the promises are "the sure mercies of David." John Wesley gathers the covenant's marks in a phrase David's dying faith could rest on: it is "ordered, well ordered in all things... sure... all our salvation," and therefore "all our desire" — and he insists it "refers also to the covenant of grace made with all believers." Clarke agrees that all is firm "relative to my spiritual successor, though he do not as yet appear."

Matthew Henry, whose meditation on this verse runs through his whole comment, sets out its five securities — the covenant is everlasting, ordered, sure, all our salvation, and all our desire — and grounds David's comfort in its being lodged "not in our own keeping, but in the keeping of a Mediator." He and Gill both look past "the covenant of royalty" typified here to "the covenant of grace made with all believers," the abiding thing when a house is "diminished, distressed, disgraced."

So the debate is only over the tone of David's voice — whether he sighs a concession ("although") or asks a confident question ("is not?"). Either way the resolution is the same: over against a family that failed the cloudless morning of v.4, God has set a covenant that cannot fail, and the dying king stakes everything upon it. What withers in his sons abides in his Seed.
6:
Against the everlasting covenant the oracle sets its dark counterpart — the doom of the sons of Belial, likened to thorns — and the witnesses work out both the philology and the reference. The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the term. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the word stands here in the abstract, "worthlessness," which is "stronger than the worthless man," and "depicts the godless as personified worthlessness"; the thorns in view are not those men avoid for their prickles but "thorns that men root out." Charles Ellicott agrees that the Masoretic pointing gives the abstract, "worthlessness," and draws a striking moral law from it: "the coming in of Divine righteousness leads not only to the assimilation of that which is holy, but also to the rejection of that which is evil, by a law as necessary and immutable as that of action and reaction in the material world."

The Reformed Gill spreads the reference wide. The thorn, "useless, hurtful, and pernicious, and fit only for burning," images "wicked men in general, that cast off the yoke of the Lord" — for Belial, he notes, signifies the unprofitable, the good-for-nothing — and "wicked governors in particular," who instead of being their people's joy "give pain and trouble, and cause grief and sorrow," and so, "if possible, are to be thrust away, and deposed from government." John Wesley reads the verse structurally: having described "the nature and stability of that kingdom which God had by a sure covenant settled," David "now describes the nature and miserable condition of all the enemies of this holy and blessed kingdom," whom God "will thrust away from himself, and from his people, and kingdom."

The Wesleyan Clarke supplies the one distinct reading. Noting that "there is no word in the text for sons — it is simply Belial, the good-for-nothing man," he ventures that it "may here refer first to Saul, and secondly to the enemies of our Lord," and he unfolds the figure from husbandry: a metaphor "taken from hedging," where the workman "thrusts the thorns aside... till, getting a fair blow at the roots, he cuts them all down."

The disagreement is only over scope — whether Belial names a general class of the wicked (Gill, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley) or points more narrowly at Saul and Christ's foes (Clarke). On the thing itself the witnesses are one: what cannot be handled must be handled with iron, and the burning of v.7 follows of course. The morning that blesses the righteous consumes the bramble.
7:
The doom of the thorns is now completed: they cannot be gripped by the bare hand but must be met "with iron and the staff of a spear," and then "utterly burned with fire in the same place." The witnesses read the verse as a picture of judgment that is at once careful, necessary, and total. John Gill takes the arming two ways — either the weapons needed "to remove these thorns, or sons of Belial, out of the way," or the hurt they inflict, so that "a man that meddles with them must expect to be as much hurt and wounded... as if the point or iron head of a spear, and the wood or handle of the spear, were thrust up in him"; but he judges "the former sense seems best." Evil, that is, must be dealt with in armour, never with a naked hand.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott does the close textual work. "The thorns are to be handled with an iron hook on the end of a spear staff," he explains, and he flags the obscure closing phrase, "in the same place," used "only here," whose meaning "is quite uncertain": the Vulgate renders it "to nothing," meaning "utter destruction," the LXX substitutes "shame," and the English "is as well sustained as any." He adds that the Chaldee Targum on these verses is "very interesting," giving the whole a Messianic application. Keil and Delitzsch, having explained the rooting-out of the brambles, let the verse turn the page — for with it the oracle ends and the roll of David's heroes begins.

Matthew Henry gathers the pastoral uses. The burning of the thorns "with unquenchable fire" (he hears the echo of the ground that "beareth thorns," Heb. 6:8) is at once "a direction to magistrates to use their power for the punishing and suppressing of wickedness," "a caution to David's sons and successors" not to be found sons of Belial themselves, and "a prediction of the ruin of all the implacable enemies of Christ's kingdom" — both open foes and secret traitors — whom Christ "will go through" as fire through briers (Isa. 27:4), until his church has "no pricking brier nor grieving thorn," and those who will not repent are burned (Luke 19:27).

There is no real dispute here, only a shared solemnity. The same oracle that promised the righteous a cloudless morning warns the worthless of an unquenchable fire, and the two ends stand together as the last testimony of a dying king. With the word "in the same place" the song falls silent, and the historian turns to name the men whose iron served that kingdom.
8:
The roll of David's heroes opens, and this single verse becomes the chapter's densest textual battleground. The Wesleyan Clarke, following Kennicott, declares that "this one verse contains three great corruptions in the Hebrew text": the hero's proper name, Jashobeam, has been turned into common words, "that sat in the seat"; the phrase "he lift up his spear" has been twisted into a second proper name, "Adino the Eznite"; and "eight hundred" was "probably at first three hundred, as in 1 Chron. 11:11." He even suspects chalal, "slain," should be "soldiers," yielding "he withstood three hundred soldiers at one time."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott concurs on the main points — the clause "should read, Jashobeam the Hachmonite," and "the same was Adino the Eznite" is "generally considered a corruption" of "he lifted up his spear" — while quietly parting from Clarke on the number: "the probability is in favour of the correctness of the text here," namely eight hundred, slain "in one combat." He notes that "the word for captain and the word for three are much alike," so the text "perpetually fluctuates between the two," and that Joab is nowhere in the list because, "as commander-in-chief, he stood in a rank by himself." Keil and Delitzsch add that Chronicles heads the list with "the number of the mighty men," a corps "named from the number of which it originally consisted," and treats Josheb-basshebeth as a spurious reading for Jashobeam.

The Reformed Gill supplies the framework the rest presuppose: the heroes fall into "three classes" — a first three who were "general officers," a second three, and "the third of thirty," the captains — with Joab, the general, standing outside them all. John Wesley handles the difficulty another way, reconciling this catalogue with the fuller one in Chronicles by two homely rules: "nothing is more common than for one person to have divers names," and "as some of the worthies died, and others came in their stead," the later list naturally differs from this earlier one.

The Puritan Matthew Henry lifts the whole roster above its textual thickets. The catalogue stands "for the honour of David," who trained such men; "for the honour of those worthies," who brought him to the crown; "to excite those that come after to a generous emulation"; and "to show how much religion contributes to the inspiring of men with true courage" — for David "greatly promoted piety among the grandees of the kingdom, and, when they became famous for piety, they became famous for bravery." So the two threads of the verse — a corrupted name and a mighty deed — meet in one point: whatever the copyists blurred, the Spirit meant these men to be remembered.
9:
The second of the first three, Eleazar the son of Dodo the Ahohite, is introduced by his finest hour — a stand against the Philistines "when the men of Israel were gone away" — and the witnesses spend their care on where and how it happened. Adam Clarke connects the scene to Israel's most famous Philistine war, supposing it "to refer to the war in which David slew Goliath." Charles Ellicott makes that link exact through the text of Chronicles: the phrase "there gathered" demands "the mention of some place," and the parallel reads "at Pas-dammim," which is "the Ephes-dammim of 1 Samuel 17:1, where Goliath defied the armies of Israel, and was slain by David." On this ground Eleazar's exploit stands on the very field of the giant's fall.

Ellicott also corrects the English at a second point. "Were gone away," he argues, should rather be "were gathered to battle" — "so it is translated in the LXX., Vulg., and Syriac, and so the Hebrew requires." He traces the mistake to a chain of copying in which Chronicles omits Shammah's battle, so that "the people fled from before the Philistines" became wrongly attached here, and "our translators were probably misled" by following Josephus. John Wesley, by contrast, keeps the received sense and glosses it: "gone away" means "fled away," "being dismayed at the approach of their enemies," and he cites 1 Chronicles 11:13 in support. So the verse carries a small, honest disagreement — did Israel flee, leaving Eleazar alone (Wesley, the English), or merely gather to battle (Ellicott, the versions)?

The grammatical-historical Keil settles the name and the syntax rather than the drama. He restores "the three heroes" against a defective reading, notes that Dodo and Dodai are "only a variation in the form of the name," and observes that "the son of an Ahohite is the same as the Ahohite," so the disputed ben need not be struck out. John Gill, briefest of all, simply fixes Eleazar's rank — "the next to the Tachmonite, the second worthy of the first class" — and links "Ahohite" to Ahoah, a descendant of Benjamin.

Whichever way the crowd behaved, the point of the verse survives every variant: one man of the first three is remembered for standing when standing was hard. The full weight of his solitary courage lands in the next verse, where his hand cleaves to the sword; here the witnesses only set the stage — a Benjamite of Ahoah's line, on the field where a shepherd once felled a giant, refusing to move.
10:
Eleazar's stand now reaches its climax: "he smote the Philistines until his hand was weary, and his hand clave unto the sword," while "the LORD wrought a great victory," and "the people returned after him only to spoil." The witnesses read the verse as a portrait of valour that gives all the glory to God. John Gill sketches the bare scene — "he rose up when the Israelites fled, and stood his ground alone, and fought with the Philistines... until his hand was weary with smiting." Charles Ellicott lingers on the strange detail of the hand that "clave unto the sword": instances are "rare, but well authenticated, of a sort of cramp following excessive exertion, so that the hand could only be released from the sword by external appliances." He also guards Eleazar's comrades from slander — "returned after him" "does not imply that they had at any time deserted him, but only that they turned wherever he went to gather the spoil of the men he slew."

The Puritan Matthew Henry mines the picture for its lesson. Eleazar's "hand was weary, and yet it clave to his sword; as long as he had any strength remaining he held his weapon and followed his blow." From this Henry draws the soldier's spiritual likeness: "in the service of God, we should keep up the willingness and resolution of the spirit, notwithstanding the weakness and weariness of the flesh — faint, yet pursuing" (Judg. 8:4), "the hand weary, yet not quitting the sword." He notes too the shrewd human touch that those who fled "returned to spoil," for "it is common for those who quit the field... to hasten to it when any thing is to be gotten."

Yet Henry and Gill will not let the hero eclipse the Lord. Twice in these battles, Henry observes, "it is here said, The Lord wrought a great victory": "how great soever the bravery of the instruments is, the praise of the achievement must be given to God. These fought the battles, but God wrought the victory." He turns it into the apostle's rule — "let not the strong man glory in his strength... but let him that glories glory in the Lord" (1 Cor. 1:31). Keil and Delitzsch, meanwhile, keep the narrative moving, using this verse to bring on the third hero, Shammah.

The voices do not contend. They agree that Eleazar's cramped and cleaving hand is the very emblem of persevering courage, and that the God who "wrought a great victory" is the one the whole roll finally means to praise. The man's strength failed at the wrist; the victory did not fail, because it was never merely the man's.
11:
The third of the first three, Shammah the son of Agee the Hararite, wins his name over an unlikely prize — "a piece of ground full of lentiles" — when "the people fled from the Philistines" but he did not. The witnesses attend to the place, the crop, and a small harmonizing puzzle. John Gill reads "Hararite" as "one who was of the mountainous country... the hill country of Judea," and distinguishes this Shammah from another of the same name among the thirty. Charles Ellicott explains the setting: the Philistines "had made a foray to gather the ripe crops, the Israelites were terrified and fled, while Shammah, by his courage and valour, turned the tide of battle, and won a great victory." He notes that Josephus, "using different vowels," read the word for "troop" as "to Lehi," the scene of Samson's exploit (Judg. 15:9), but since the same word recurs plainly as "troop" in v.13, "the English reading should be retained."

The one textual wrinkle is the crop. Where this verse has lentiles, 1 Chronicles 11:13 has barley. Adam Clarke thinks it "a mistake of adashim, lentiles, for seorim, barley, or vice versa," but adds a happier possibility: "some think there were both lentiles and barley in the field," and a marauding party came to carry them off, "and these worthies defeated the whole, and saved the produce of the field. This is not unlikely." Ellicott, for his part, judges the difference "quite immaterial" — "the point is that the Philistines had made a foray to gather the ripe crops."

The Wesleyan Wesley uses the same double crop to solve a harder harmony. In Chronicles the feat is bound up with Eleazar and speaks of partners who "set themselves" in the field; here it belongs to Shammah. Wesley reconciles them cheerfully: "both might grow in the same field, in divers parts of it," so "Eleazar might fight in that part where the barley was, and Shammah where the lentiles were." The heroes, that is, held adjoining ground in one battle.

Under the philology the verse carries a quiet dignity the witnesses feel without labouring it: a warrior of the first rank spends his courage not on a city or a crown but on a bean-field, refusing to let the enemy plunder even so small a portion of the promised land. What the plunderer would strip, the hero stands to keep — and, as v.12 will insist, the LORD wrought the victory.
12:
The account of Shammah closes with the deed itself: "he stood in the midst of the ground, and defended it, and slew the Philistines: and the LORD wrought a great victory." The witnesses are few and brief here, and they let the verse speak for its own three verbs — stood, defended, slew. John Gill fixes the scene with a single phrase, "the field of lentiles," so that the ground so hotly held is the very bean-plot of the previous verse — a patch of tillage most men would have abandoned without shame. Shammah plants himself "in the midst" of it and will not be moved.

The stress of the witnesses falls, as it did at v.10, on the closing clause. John Wesley makes it the whole point of the verse: "the Lord wrought — how great soever the bravery of the instruments is, the praise of the achievement is to be given to God. These fought, but God wrought the victory." The same refrain that crowned Eleazar's stand now crowns Shammah's, and the repetition is plainly deliberate: the historian will not let two solitary heroes in a row be admired without naming the unseen hand that made them stand. Keil and Delitzsch, having settled the field and the fleeing people, use the verse to turn from the first three to a fresh exploit — the three nameless men who will go down to the cave of Adullam in v.13.

Thin as the comment is, the verse rewards a closer look the witnesses invite rather than supply. What Shammah defends is a triviality — lentils, not a fortress; yet in the economy of the promised land no portion is trivial, for every furrow of it is covenant ground, and to yield a bean-field to the Philistine is to concede that the enemy may feed where God has planted his people. Shammah's lonely stand is therefore of a piece with Eleazar's cramped hand: both men treat a small thing as worth dying for, because the God of Israel is behind it.

And both exploits end at the same confession. Twice the sentence falls — "the LORD wrought a great victory" — and the twin heroes of verses ten and twelve become, between them, a single sermon: the courage is real and honoured, but the deliverance is God's. Human valour holds the ground; divine power routs the foe. The roll of the mighty, even at its most heroic, keeps pointing past the men to the Rock of Israel who fought through them.
13:
The narrative now steps back from single champions to a joint exploit of "three of the thirty," who "went down... to David in the harvest time unto the cave of Adullam" while "the troop of the Philistines pitched in the valley of Rephaim." The witnesses divide, gently, over which three. John Gill records the two readings and picks one: some take these to be "three of the thirty," and others "the three before mentioned" — Josephus among them — so that, having told their several deeds, the historian now relates "one in which they were all concerned"; Gill judges "the first sense is best," namely the famous first three. Charles Ellicott comes down the other way: since the Hebrew has no article, "these are not the same three... but were another three more eminent than the rest of the thirty," probably "Abishai and Benaiah" among them. Keil and Delitzsch agree with Ellicott — "not the three already mentioned, but three others out of the number."

The grammatical-historical commentators also weigh the shifting word "thirty." Adam Clarke suspects the term shalishim, which we translate "thirty," "probably signifies an office or particular description of men," not a fixed count — the more so since the chapter finally lists thirty-seven, and "it can scarcely be said... that we have thirty-seven out of thirty." He recalls that Pharaoh's chariot-captains bear the same title (Exod. 14:7). Ellicott reads "the thirty" as "a common name for this band of heroes," originally perhaps exactly thirty but "whose number varied from time to time," here reaching thirty-seven (v.39).

Two smaller notes round out the verse. On "in the harvest time," Ellicott passes on Kirkpatrick's caution that "the preposition does not mean in," and that Chronicles' reading "to the rock" may be the true one. On the "valley of Rephaim," Clarke identifies the campaign with the Philistine war of 2 Samuel 5, and Ellicott sends the reader to the same place. Keil and Delitzsch fix the refuge as the cave of Adullam, the old hold of David's outlaw days (1 Samuel 22:1).

The disagreement over identity is not idle, for it colours the story that follows: if the three are the very first of David's champions, their coming down is the more remarkable; if they are three of the thirty, the exploit that made them famous is all the more daring. Either way the scene is now set — David in the hold, the enemy in the valley, and Bethlehem, as the next verse tells, in Philistine hands.
14:
A single sentence of scene-setting — "David was then in an hold, and the garrison of the Philistines was then in Bethlehem" — the witnesses read for its geography and, still more, for its pathos. John Gill weighs the two candidates for the "hold": either "the strong hold of Zion, as Josephus," or a fastness "on a rock near the cave of Adullam." Keil and Delitzsch use the verse to launch the whole episode that follows, describing how "the three heroes then broke through the camp of the Philistines at Bethlehem, i.e., the outpost that occupied the space before the gate," fetched the water, and brought it to a king who "poured it out upon the ground to the Lord, as a drink-offering." The hold and the garrison are thus the two poles of a daring errand — David pent up in his refuge, the enemy lodged in his birthplace.

The Puritan Matthew Henry feels the wound in the geography. That "the Philistines pitched in the valley of Rephaim, and put a garrison even in Bethlehem itself," is for him the bitter fruit of Saul's misrule: "if the church's guides are so misled as to persecute some of her best friends and champions, the common enemy will, no doubt, get advantage by it." Had David enjoyed his liberty rather than being driven "to shelter themselves from Saul's rage in caves and strong holds," Henry reasons, "Bethlehem would not have been now in the Philistines' hands." The enemy holds the town of promise only because Israel's anointed is an outlaw in a cave.

There is no debate among so brief and agreed a company; the verse is a hinge, and its whole force is in the contrast it draws. Bethlehem — the city of Ruth and of Jesse, David's own home, and one day the birthplace of David's greater Son — lies under a Philistine garrison, while the man born there crouches in a mountain hold within sight of it. The very ground of his childhood, the well by whose gate he had drunk as a boy, is now behind enemy lines.

That contrast is exactly what makes the next verse intelligible. David's sudden longing for "the water of the well of Bethlehem" is not a random thirst but a homesick king's cry for a taste of the place the enemy has taken from him. The witnesses set the stage carefully in this verse so that the longing, the daring, and the self-denial of verses fifteen through seventeen may land with full weight: everything turns on Bethlehem being both dear and, for the moment, lost.
15:
David's sudden cry — "Oh that one would give me drink of the water of the well of Bethlehem, which is by the gate!" — the witnesses read as a very human longing, and they differ over how to judge it. John Gill and John Wesley take it at face value: it was "harvest time, the summer season, and hot weather, and he thirsty," and Wesley adds that David, "being hot and thirsty, expresses how acceptable a draught of that water would be to him," yet "was far from desiring, or expecting, that any of his men should hazard their lives to procure it." Adam Clarke agrees the wish was natural — Bethlehem "was David's city, and he knew the excellence of the water which was there; and being near the place, and parched with thirst, it was natural for him to wish for a draught" — and he is careful that "there is no evidence that David had requested" it; the three "had gone for it of their own accord."

The Puritan Matthew Henry probes the longing more searchingly, and lays three readings side by side. Some, he says, make it "a public-spirited wish" — that David meant, "O that we could drive the garrison of the Philistines out of Bethlehem, and make that beloved city of mine our own again!" — the well standing for the city. But since "those about him did not understand him" so, Henry judges it "rather an instance of his weakness": a thirsty man's fancy for the water of his boyhood, when "other water might quench his thirst as well," so that it is "folly to entertain such fancies, and greater folly to insist upon the gratification of them." Yet he preserves a third, tenderer reading from Bishop Patrick — that some "think it was not material water he longed for, but the Messiah, who had the water of life, who, he knew, should be born at Bethlehem."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott, meanwhile, turns to the well itself, and reports the traveller's difficulty: "there are now no wells of living water at Bethlehem itself," Robinson "could find none in the neighbourhood," yet Ritter says that "a little north of the town is David's well, with its deep shaft and its clear cool water." The place David pined for may still be seen.

So the debate is over the heart of the wish — mere appetite and weakness (Henry, and by implication Clarke), a patriot's yearning to reclaim his city (Henry, reported), or a mystical thirst for the Messiah of Bethlehem (Bishop Patrick). What none of them dispute is the sequel: whatever David meant, three men will risk their lives on the strength of a sigh, and the king will refuse to drink what they bring.
16:
The three answer the sigh with their lives: they "brake through the host of the Philistines," drew the water "out of the well of Bethlehem, that was by the gate," and brought it to David — who "would not drink thereof, but poured it out unto the LORD." The witnesses marvel first at the deed and then, more, at the refusal. John Gill notes that the three "hearing David express himself" so, "though without any view that any should risk their lives," at once "set out, and made their way through the army of the Philistines to the well." Charles Ellicott sees in it the measure of the man they served: "the brave act of the three heroes shows strikingly the personal power of David over his followers, and the enthusiasm with which he inspired them." Yet "on the other hand, David would not suffer his own longing to be gratified by the hazard of men's lives."

On the pouring-out the witnesses are agreed and eloquent. Ellicott observes that the verb "is the technical term for the sacrificial libation," so that David "assimilated his act to a sacrifice by a solemn consecration of this dangerously won water to the Lord." John Wesley reads the same motive: David "would not drink, lest by gratifying himself upon such terms he should seem either to set too high a price upon the satisfaction of his appetite, or too low a price upon the lives of his soldiers," and so he "poured it out as a kind of drink-offering, and acknowledgment of God's goodness in preserving the lives of his captains in so dangerous an enterprise." Keil and Delitzsch quote Clericus to the same end — "he poured it out... rendering Him thanks for the return of the three brave men."

The Wesleyan Clarke supplies the memorable parallel that makes the act shine. Libations "both of water and wine," he recalls, were common among the heathen, and he tells how Alexander, when "his army was greatly oppressed with heat and thirst" and a soldier brought him a cup, "ordered it to be carried back, saying, I cannot bear to drink alone while so many are in want." The example was noble in both men, Clarke grants, "but David added piety to bravery; he poured it out unto the Lord."

There is no contention here, only a shared wonder. Matthew Henry gathers the motives — David would show "the tender regard he had to the lives of his soldiers," would "testify his sorrow for speaking that foolish word," would cross "his own foolish fancy," and would "honour God" with a draught too precious for any throat but the altar. What men won at the price of blood, the king gave back to God; the sanctity of that blood is the very thing his words in v.17 will name.
17:
David's own words explain the strange libation: "Be it far from me, O LORD, that I should do this: is not this the blood of the men that went in jeopardy of their lives?" The witnesses fasten on the identification of the water with blood, and on the reverence that would not drink it. Charles Ellicott does the grammatical work: the Hebrew is not a full sentence but "an interrogative exclamation — 'the blood of the men?'" — while 1 Chronicles 11:19 fills it out, "Shall I drink the blood of these men?" To David, Ellicott explains, the water "gained only at the risk of life seemed the very blood in which the life resides," and he hears behind the words the law that the life is in the blood (Leviticus 17:11). He adds the small textual note that "these three" should read "the three."

The other voices confirm the reading. John Gill paraphrases the refusal simply — "be it far from me, O Lord, that one should do this," that is, drink "of the water these men had brought." John Wesley attends to the arithmetic of the passage, observing that "these three" acted "jointly," after which "two of them are mentioned severally" — a note that sets up the individual exploits of Abishai and Benaiah to come. Keil and Delitzsch, having rendered David's oath, use the verse to close the first exploit ("these things did these three mighty men") and to open the second class of heroes with Abishai.

There is no debate here, and the united sense is weighty. David treats the water as sacramental precisely because it cost what is sacred — the lives, and so the "blood," of men who "went in jeopardy" for a word of his. To drink it would be to spend their peril on his palate; to pour it out before the LORD is to confess that such a cost belongs to God alone. The king who once poured out other men's blood carelessly, in the matter of Uriah, here will not so much as drink the symbol of it.

That restraint is the moral heart of the whole Bethlehem episode. The valour of the three is answered not by indulgence but by worship; the longing that set them running is disciplined by a conscience that reckons a soldier's life too dear to squander on a whim. What the witnesses draw from it, without needing to argue, is a portrait of leadership sanctified — a great man who has learned to deny himself the very thing he most desired, and to hand it up, unspent, to the God whose it was. The self-command of v.16 here finds its confession.
18:
With the Bethlehem exploit told, the roll turns to its second rank, and to Abishai, the brother of Joab, the son of Zeruiah, "chief among the three," who "lifted up his spear against three hundred" and "had the name among three." The witnesses set his rank and read his deed. John Gill marks him "the head" of "another triumvirate" — the second three, standing below the first. Charles Ellicott resolves the numbers: "among three" should be "the three," meaning "the triad of heroes just mentioned, of whom Abishai was first, Benaiah second, with an unnamed third," and he compares "a somewhat similar feat of daring" told of Abishai in 1 Samuel 26:6-12, when he went down by night into Saul's camp.

The grammatical-historical Keil does the close textual repair. Abishai, he notes, "was also chief of the body-guard, like Jashobeam," and "swung his spear over three hundred slain." As for the phrase that follows — that he "had a name among the three" — Keil takes it to mean "the three principal heroes, Jashobeam, Eleazar, and Shammah," while the further words that make no clear sense, min-hashsheloshah, he judges "an error in writing" for "the thirty," as the parallel in v.23 shows. The reputation, that is, is measured against two different bands, and the copyists have blurred which.

The interest of the verse, then, is largely one of gradation. Abishai belongs to a second triad, is its chief, and yet — as the next verse will insist — does not reach the first three. His three hundred slain fall short of Jashobeam's eight hundred, and the very form of his honour ("a name among the three") keeps him at once near the summit and below it. The roll is careful to rank as well as to praise.

What the witnesses leave largely unspoken, the reader of Samuel supplies: this Abishai is no stranger, but the fierce and faithful son of Zeruiah who dogged David's whole career — ready to smite Saul in the cave, loyal in the flight from Absalom, too violent for David's taste yet never disloyal. To find him here, spear lifted over three hundred, is to see the man's ungovernable courage set at last in its proper frame, honoured among the mighty though not crowned among the first. His deeds against the enemy, unlike his deeds against David's foes at court, earn him an untarnished place in the record.
19:
The verse presses the question of Abishai's exact standing: "Was he not most honourable of three? therefore he was their captain: howbeit he attained not unto the first three." The witnesses read it as a careful piece of ranking — great, but not greatest. John Gill answers the opening question with a full account of the man's career, for Abishai "was" indeed most honourable: "besides the exploit here mentioned," he "went down with David into Saul's camp, and took away his spear and cruse" (1 Samuel 26); he "relieved David when in danger from Ishbibenob the giant" (2 Samuel 21:17); and he "beat the Edomites, and slew eighteen thousand of them in the valley of salt." His honour is no empty title but a life of rescues and victories.

The Wesleyan Wesley states the limit plainly. If Abishai "attained not" to the first three, it is because "he fell short of them in strength and valour" — a candid concession that the roll does not flatter. There is a hierarchy of heroism, and even the captain of the second triad stands a rung below Jashobeam, Eleazar, and Shammah. Keil and Delitzsch, treating verses eighteen and nineteen together, confirm the structure: Abishai heads the second class, "more honourable than the thirty," yet "attained not to the first three," and this same formula will presently be applied to Benaiah as well.

The witnesses do not dispute the ranking; they underline it. And in underlining it they teach, almost in passing, a lesson the Puritan Matthew Henry had drawn from the whole catalogue: "all great men are not of the same size. Many a bright and benign star there is which is not of the first magnitude, and many a good ship not of the first rate." Abishai is a star, but not of the first magnitude; a captain, but not one of the peerless three. The Spirit's honesty in grading his servants is itself instructive — degrees of gift and valour are real, and to be a leader of the second rank is honour enough.

There is, too, a gentle providence in the placing. Abishai's fiercest impulses had more than once needed David's restraint, yet here his courage is simply owned and measured, "their captain" though not of the first three. The record neither exaggerates nor withholds. It gives the son of Zeruiah his due — a name second only to the greatest — and then moves on, in v.20, to the other pillar of that second rank, Benaiah, whose exploits will match and in daring perhaps exceed his own.
20:
Benaiah's portrait is the roll's most vivid, and the witnesses relish its three feats: he "slew two lionlike men of Moab," and "went down also and slew a lion in the midst of a pit in time of snow." John Gill begins with the father — "a man of great vivacity, valour, and strength, so that it was like father like son" — and passes on the tradition from Procopius that Benaiah "was David's brother's son, and a grandson of Jesse." Charles Ellicott fills in the man's later greatness: Benaiah was "general of the third division of the army," commander of "the Cherethites and Pelethites" (2 Samuel 8:18), and, "in consequence of his faithfulness to Solomon in the rebellion of Adonijah, was finally made commander-in-chief" (1 Kings 2). His home Kabzeel lay "on the extreme south of Judah" (Joshua 15:21), and his father Jehoiada is called "a chief priest."

The one real question is the identity of the "two lionlike men of Moab." Adam Clarke lays out the options: "some think that two real lions are meant; some that they were two savage gigantic men; others, that two fortresses are meant." The Hebrew, he notes — sheney ariel Moab — the Targum renders "the two princes of Moab." Ellicott and Gill lean the same way: ariel, "lion of God," is "an expression used among Arabs and Persians of great warriors," so the phrase most likely marks "two celebrated Moabitish heroes" rather than beasts or towers.

On the lion in the pit the witnesses add color. Ellicott simply compares the young David's own lion (1 Samuel 17:34-37), noting that "it is not said with what weapons he slew him, but the act was evidently a great feat of valour." John Wesley draws out the peculiar danger of the setting: in "a pit" Benaiah "put himself under a necessity, either of killing, or being killed," with no room to flee; and the season, "of snow," was the time "when lions are most fierce, both from the sharpness of their appetite in cold seasons, and from want of provisions." Every circumstance heightens the hazard — a cornered man, a starving beast, a slippery floor.

The debate over the "lionlike men" is friendly and largely settled toward "warriors," and it hardly dims the verse. What the witnesses leave standing is a hero of almost reckless bravery — slayer of Moab's champions, lion-killer in a snow-choked cistern — whose valour, unlike mere ferocity, would prove itself as loyal as it was fierce, and would carry him at last to the head of Solomon's army. The single-handed feat that seals his fame waits in v.21.
21:
Benaiah's crowning exploit sets a lightly armed Israelite against a towering foe: he "slew an Egyptian, a goodly man," who "had a spear in his hand," yet Benaiah "went down to him with a staff, and plucked the spear out of the Egyptian's hand, and slew him with his own spear." The witnesses first take the measure of the giant. John Gill and Charles Ellicott both draw on Chronicles, where "a goodly man" is explained as "a man of stature, five cubits high" — Gill reckoning him only "a cubit and a span" short of Goliath's height (1 Samuel 17:4). Ellicott states the feat plainly: Benaiah's greatness lay in "coming, armed only with a staff, to this giant Egyptian, wresting his spear from him, and then slaying him with it."

The Wesleyan Clarke, delighting in the picture, supplies the roll's most famous outside parallel. He tells at length, from Quintus Curtius, of the duel before Alexander between "Dioxippus the Athenian" and "Horratas a Macedonian": the Macedonian came "armed cap-a-pie," with shield, spear, javelin and sword, while the Athenian entered "his body naked, smeared over with oil, and in his right hand a strong knotty club." The naked man dodged the javelin, shattered the spear with one blow, seized his armoured foe, "tripped up his heels," and set his foot on his neck. "How similar are the two cases!" Clarke exclaims; "Benaiah appears to have been just such another clubsman." The lesson is that skill and nerve can undo a whole arsenal — a man with a staff mastering a man with a spear.

The grammatical-historical Keil keeps the ledger, noting that by this and his other deeds Benaiah "had the name among three mighty men," was "more honourable than the thirty," though "he attained not to the first three," and that "David set him over his guard." The exploit thus earns him the same measured honour already given Abishai — chief of the second rank, captain of the bodyguard, but not of the peerless three.

No one disputes the reading; the witnesses simply admire. And the alert reader hears the deliberate echo the text is sounding: as the boy David once went out against the Philistine "with a staff" and a sling and felled the armoured Goliath with his own weapon, so now David's captain goes down "with a staff" against a near-giant Egyptian and kills him with his own spear. The pattern of the king reappears in the servant. What David was in the valley of Elah, Benaiah is in his turn — proof that the courage which first won the crown had multiplied itself among the men who guarded it.
22:
This brief closing note on Benaiah — "These things did Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and had the name among three mighty men" — carries only John Gill's single sentence of comment, yet the verse is doing real work in the structure of the roll, and rewards the drawing-out the witnesses invite. Gill's gloss is simply to gather the deeds: Benaiah "slew a lion, and two lion-like men of Moab, and an Egyptian of a gigantic stature." The verse is a summing-up, a setting of the seal on the exploits just told.

Its force lies in the phrase "had the name." The whole catalogue is, at bottom, a book of names — men remembered because the Spirit chose to remember them — and here that theme rises to the surface. Benaiah's several feats are collected under a single honour: he "had the name among three mighty men." It is not the man's strength that the verse finally records but his renown, the reputation that his strength earned and that Scripture now preserves. The deeds pass; the name abides, written into the sacred history where it will outlast every Moabite champion and Egyptian giant he ever met.

The verse also fixes Benaiah's precise standing, echoing the formula already used of Abishai and about to be repeated: he had a name "among three," reckoned with the mighty yet, as the next verse insists, not numbered with the first three. The roll is scrupulous about degree. It will not inflate Benaiah into a rival of Jashobeam or Eleazar, nor let his dazzling exploits blur the ranks; it honours him exactly, "among three mighty men," and no higher.

Read against the whole chapter, this small verse quietly states the logic of the entire list. David's kingdom was upheld not by the king alone but by named men, each honoured for what he did, each set in his proper place. Benaiah's summary is a specimen of the whole: valour recorded, reputation secured, rank observed. And it points forward — for the honour just affirmed is the very thing the next verse will qualify and reward, when David, prizing such a man, "set him over his guard." The name won in battle becomes the trust granted at court; the reputation of this verse becomes the office of the next.
23:
The verse ranks Benaiah and rewards him: "He was more honourable than the thirty, but he attained not to the first three. And David set him over his guard." The witnesses agree on the standing and puzzle over the office. John Gill takes the rank at face value — Benaiah "was more honourable than the thirty, whose names are after recorded" — placing him, with Abishai, in the elite second rank, above the general body but below the peerless first three. The formula is by now familiar, and its repetition fixes the architecture of the whole roll: three at the summit, three (or their survivors) just beneath, and thirty below them.

The interest gathers on the last clause, "David set him over his guard," where the witnesses divide over a single Hebrew word. Adam Clarke reports the range: the Vulgate renders it "David made him his privy counsellor," or, "according to the Hebrew, He put him to his ears" — that is, "confided his secrets to him"; while "some think he made him a spy over the rest," and Clarke recalls the fable that Midas's long ears meant a king who "carried the system of espionage to a great length." Charles Ellicott presses the same doubt: "the word translated guard means rather private audience," so that "David either made him a member of, or set him over his council," and he notes that if 1 Chronicles 27:34 has transposed a name, Benaiah's holding of this office "is also mentioned there."

So a genuine, if minor, debate stands: did David set Benaiah over his bodyguard (the English, and the plain sense of a captain of the Cherethites and Pelethites), or over his council, admitting him to the king's private ear (Vulgate, Clarke, Ellicott)? The two are not far apart in a court where the captain of the guard was also the man closest to the throne, and the witnesses do not force a choice.

Either way the verse completes Benaiah's story on a note of trust. The valour proved in a snowy pit and against an Egyptian giant is now translated into nearness to the king — whether guarding his person or sharing his secrets. And the placing is prophetic: this is the man who, faithful to David's line when Adonijah grasped at the crown, would become Solomon's commander-in-chief. The honour recorded here — second only to the first three, and set over the king's most intimate charge — is the seed of the greatness Benaiah would reach in the next reign. From this trust the roll turns, in v.24, to name the thirty themselves.
24:
The list of the thirty now begins, and its first name carries a shadow: "Asahel the brother of Joab was one of the thirty; Elhanan the son of Dodo of Bethlehem." The witnesses read the opening for what it reveals about the whole catalogue's date. John Gill weighs whether Asahel was "one of the thirty" or rather "over the thirty," since the Arabic calls him "the prince of the thirty," and repeats that Joab, "general of the whole army," is "not named at all" in any class. Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott both remember Asahel's fate: he "was the same that was killed by Abner" (2 Samuel 2:23), and Ellicott draws the chronological inference — since Asahel fell "while David reigned over Judah only, it is plain that this list is not restricted to any one definite time in David's reign." Leaving Asahel aside, he adds, "the names that follow are exactly thirty."

The grammatical-historical commentators also sort the names. Ellicott notes that "of but few of them is anything further known," and Keil and Delitzsch distinguish this Elhanan "of Bethlehem" from the Elhanan of 2 Samuel 21:19, a different man of the same name. The roll, that is, is a real muster of real men, some famous and most obscure, gathered "from all parts of the nation."

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the theology of the list from this very opening. That Asahel heads it though long dead — "slain by Abner in the beginning of David's reign, but lost not his place in this catalogue" — teaches Henry that honour once earned in God's service is not cancelled by death. He observes that the surnames "are taken, as it should seem, from the places of their birth or habitation," and that these men were the seedbed of David's standing army, several of them "captains of the twelve courses" (1 Chronicles 27). And he lifts the whole roll to its antitype: "Christ, the Son of David, has his worthies too," his apostles named with honour (Revelation 21:14), and all his good soldiers whose "names are written in heaven" (Luke 10:20).

There is no debate; the witnesses agree that the list spans David's whole reign and that its very first entry, a hero cut down early yet still enrolled, is a parable of remembrance. What Asahel did lives on though Asahel does not — and that, Henry insists, is exactly how the King of kings keeps the record of those who fight his battles. The thirty that follow are but the filling-in of a roll of honour that death cannot erase.
25:
With Asahel entered, the muster proceeds through a file of names — here "Shammah the Harodite, Elika the Harodite" — and the witnesses take these obscurer verses as occasions for geography, for cross-reference, and for a standing rule about how such lists are to be read. John Gill begins with a philological caution: this Shammah is "called Shammah the Harorite" in the parallel place, "by a change of the letters R and D, which is frequent" in Hebrew copying. Charles Ellicott ventures a possible identification — Shammah "may be the same with Shamhuth the Izrahite, captain of the fifth division of the army" (1 Chronicles 27:8) — and notes that "the next name," Elika, "is omitted in Chronicles" altogether.

The grammatical-historical Keil, reading ahead through the file, ties several of these men to David's standing army: Helez "chief of the seventh division," Ira son of Ikkesh "chief of the sixth" — showing that the thirty were not merely past heroes but the officers of an organized force. Adam Clarke contents himself with the general warning that "there are several varieties in the names of the following shalishim, which may be seen by comparing these verses with 1 Chronicles 11:27."

It is John Wesley who supplies the interpretive frame for the entire remainder of the chapter. Faced with the tangle of differing forms, he lays down four sober principles for reconciling this catalogue with the one in Chronicles: "that the same names of persons, or places, are differently pronounced according to the different dialects of divers places or ages; that one man had often two names; that David had more worthies than those here mentioned; and that, as some of these were slain in the former part of David's reign, as Asahel was, so others came up in their stead." The variations, in short, are not errors of substance but the ordinary marks of a long reign and a living language.

There is no debate among the witnesses, only a shared method. Where a modern reader sees a dry list of half-known men, they see a real regiment of a real king, its names worn and altered by transmission yet faithfully preserved, its officers scattered "from all parts of the nation." Two men of Harod stand at the head of the thirty — one perhaps a division-captain, the other lost to Chronicles entirely — and the witnesses honour both by the patient labour of identifying them. The very obscurity of the names becomes, in their hands, a testimony that God keeps account of servants the world would never have remembered.
26:
The file continues — "Helez the Paltite, Ira the son of Ikkesh the Tekoite" — and the witnesses trace each man to his town and, where they can, to his place in David's army. John Gill notes that Helez the Paltite "was of a place called" Beth-pelet, "as the Targum," and that he "is called the Pelonite" in the parallel record — one of the frequent variations Wesley had just taught the reader to expect. Charles Ellicott fixes both men in the standing army: Helez "was general of the seventh army division" (1 Chronicles 27:10), where "he is called a Pelonite," and Ira "was general for the sixth month" (1 Chronicles 27:9). Keil and Delitzsch add the geography: Helez "sprung from Beth-pelet in the south of Judah" (Joshua 15:27), and Ira from "Tekoah in the desert of Judah."

That last name carries more resonance than its brevity suggests, and the witnesses supply the threads even if they do not weave them. Ira's home, Tekoah — Ellicott places it "about six miles south of Bethlehem" — is the town of the wise woman whom Joab fetched to plead for Absalom (2 Samuel 14:2), and long after would be the home of the herdsman-prophet Amos. From this small place in the wilderness of Judah came one of David's thirty, a captain set over a twelfth part of the army's yearly courses.

There is no dispute here; the labour of the witnesses is simply the patient work of identification, matching each worn name to a village and, through Chronicles, to a rank. Helez the Paltite and Ira of Tekoah are men of the southern hill country, drawn up into the king's service and given charge, each in his month, over the rotating divisions that guarded Israel. The roll is not a random assortment of champions but the officer-list of an ordered realm.

What the file quietly displays, as it moves from town to town, is the breadth of David's kingdom in the men who upheld it. Every verse plants another name in another place — Beth-pelet, Tekoah, and soon Anathoth and Netophah — until the thirty become a kind of map of Judah and Benjamin, the whole land represented in the guard of the anointed king. The obscurity of these captains is not neglect but inclusion: men the histories would otherwise pass over in silence are here written down, each by name and each by home, in the record of those through whom the LORD upheld David's throne. The next verse carries the muster north into Benjamin.
27:
The muster crosses into Benjamin — "Abiezer the Anethothite, Mebunnai the Hushathite" — and the witnesses locate both men and quietly enrich the first name with its later fame. John Gill marks Abiezer as one "of Anathoth, in the tribe of Benjamin," and cannot resist adding what makes that village memorable: it was "the birthplace of Jeremiah the prophet." Charles Ellicott confirms it — Abiezer "was of Anathoth, a priestly city of Benjamin, the home of Jeremiah" — and sets him in the army as "general for the ninth month" (1 Chronicles 27:12). So one of David's thirty came from the same small priestly town that would, centuries on, give Israel its weeping prophet.

The second name is a knot of transmission, and the grammatical-historical commentators untie it together. Keil and Ellicott agree that "Mebunnai" is "a mistake in spelling for Sibbechai the Hushathite" — the two forms, Ellicott notes, being "much alike in the original and easily confused." That identification recovers a real hero, for Sibbechai is the man who "slew the giant Saph" (2 Samuel 21:18) and served as "general for the eighth month" (1 Chronicles 27:11). What looks at first like an unknown soldier turns out, once the spelling is mended, to be one of the giant-killers of the Philistine wars.

There is no debate among so agreed a company; the work is recovery, not dispute. And the recovery is instructive. Behind a garbled name stands a distinguished captain; behind a bare place-name stands a town heavy with prophetic memory. The witnesses will not let either be lost. They restore Sibbechai to his exploit and give Anathoth its due, and in doing so they show what the whole roll is for — the keeping of names that a careless eye would skip.

The pairing also widens the map. Where the previous verse gathered men of the southern hills of Judah, this one plants two in Benjamin — Anathoth to the north-east of Jerusalem, Hushah near Bethlehem — so that the guard of the anointed king is seen to be drawn from tribe after tribe. A priestly village that would one day mourn under Jeremiah's laments here sends a warrior to uphold David's throne; a giant-slayer stands next to him under a mangled name. The obscurity of the verse is only apparent: read with the witnesses' care, it holds a prophet's hometown and a killer of Philistine giants, both enrolled among the mighty men of God's chosen king.
28:
Two more captains are named — "Zalmon the Ahohite, Maharai the Netophathite" — and the witnesses handle them, as they have the file throughout, with the double labour of tracing the man and reconciling the name. John Gill reports at once that Zalmon "is the same with Ilai" in the parallel record, and that he was "a descendant of Ahoah, a grandson of Benjamin" — so a second Benjamite in as many verses. Keil and Delitzsch confirm the variant honestly, adding that "which of the two names is the correct one it is impossible to decide"; the copyists have left Zalmon and Ilai standing side by side, and the witnesses do not pretend to a certainty the text will not yield.

Maharai they place more firmly. Charles Ellicott notes that "he commanded the tenth division of the army" (1 Chronicles 27:13), and Keil fixes his home: Netophah "was a place in the neighbourhood of Bethlehem," though its exact site "has not yet been discovered," and by descent Maharai "belonged to the Judahite family of Serah." A man of Judah, then, from a village near David's own birthplace, set over one of the twelve monthly courses of the army.

There is no dispute here, only the steady work of identification, and it is worth pausing on what that work assumes. The witnesses treat even a name as slippery as Zalmon-or-Ilai as worth pinning down, precisely because the roll is Scripture and every entry in it was meant to be kept. Where they cannot decide between two forms they say so plainly; where they can trace a man to his tribe, his town, and his month of command, they do it. The honesty is of a piece with the reverence: they will neither force the text nor neglect it.

And the cumulative effect, verse upon verse, is a portrait of a well-ordered kingdom. Netophah near Bethlehem, Ahoah in Benjamin — the thirty keep coming from the villages of Judah and Benjamin, each captain slotted into the rotation that guarded Israel month by month. What might read as a monotonous list is, to the witnesses' eyes, the officer-roll of a realm at rest under a shepherd-king, its every corner represented in the men who bore its arms. The next verse carries the same care to Heleb of Netophah and to an Ittai who must be kept distinct from a more famous namesake.
29:
The file names "Heleb the son of Baanah, a Netophathite," and "Ittai the son of Ribai out of Gibeah of the children of Benjamin," and the witnesses attend to a variant spelling and a needful distinction. John Gill notes only that Heleb "is called Heled" in the parallel list — another of the interchangeable forms. Keil and Delitzsch record the same man under three shapes — "Cheleb, more correctly Cheled, or Cheldai" — and set him as "chief of the twelfth division of the army," a second Netophathite following Maharai of the previous verse. Charles Ellicott agrees: the name is "variously written Heled and Heldai," and "he was the general for the twelfth month" (1 Chronicles 27:15).

The care of the witnesses falls chiefly on the second name, for there is a famous Ittai in David's story, and this is not he. Both Keil and Ellicott insist that "Ittai the son of Ribai," of "Gibeah of Benjamin," "must be distinguished from Ittai the Gittite" (2 Samuel 15:19) — the loyal foreigner who refused to leave David in the flight from Absalom. This Ittai is a Benjamite of Saul's own town, Gibeah; that Ittai was a Philistine of Gath. The two share a name and nothing else, and the witnesses will not let the reader confuse the guard-captain with the celebrated exile.

There is no debate, only the discipline of exact reading. Keil adds the sober observation that governs the whole closing stretch of the roll: "like all that follow, with the exception of Uriah," Ittai "is not further known." From here to the end the names are mostly bare — a man, a father, a town, and nothing more — and the witnesses do not manufacture stories the text withholds. They fix what can be fixed and leave the rest in the honest obscurity of the record.

Yet even that obscurity preaches. A captain from Gibeah of Benjamin — the very town of Saul, the house that had hunted David through the wilderness — now stands among David's thirty, his tribe's old enmity swallowed up in service to the anointed king. Alongside him a man of Netophah near Bethlehem takes his monthly command. The roll keeps gathering Israel from its divided places into one guard, and it does so name by unremembered name, each written down because the King through whom the LORD reigned counted no faithful servant too small to keep. The muster presses on, in the next verse, to Ephraim and the brooks of Gaash.
30:
The muster reaches Ephraim — "Benaiah the Pirathonite, Hiddai of the brooks of Gaash" — and the witnesses first guard against a confusion of names, then draw out the memories that cling to these northern places. Charles Ellicott issues the caution plainly: this Benaiah "is of course to be distinguished from Benaiah" the son of Jehoiada of v.20; he "was general for the eleventh month" (1 Chronicles 27:14), a different man of the same honoured name. The lion-slayer of the second rank and this captain of the thirty are two, and the reader must not merge them.

The grammatical-historical Keil supplies the geography, and it is richer than the bare names suggest. Benaiah came "of Phir'aton in the tribe of Ephraim," a place still "preserved in the village of Fer'ata, to the south-west of Nablus" — the same Pirathon where the judge Abdon was buried (Judges 12:15). Hiddai — whose name Chronicles gives as "Hurai," Ellicott notes, "owing to the frequent confusion of d and r" — came "out of the valleys of Gaash, in the tribe of Ephraim by the mountain of Gaash," the very hill at whose north side Joshua himself was buried (Joshua 24:30).

There is no dispute among the witnesses, only this quiet enriching of obscure names with the histories they touch. A captain of David's thirty hails from the burial-place of one of the judges; another from the mountain that holds the grave of Israel's first conqueror. The roll, read with the witnesses' learning, keeps brushing against the older story of the nation — as though David's guard were gathered from ground already hallowed by the leaders who went before.

And the northward reach of the verse matters. Where the file had lingered in Judah and Benjamin, it now names two men of Ephraim, the leading tribe of the north. The kingdom David guarded was not a southern faction but the whole of Israel, and his thirty are drawn accordingly — from Bethlehem and Tekoah in the south to Pirathon and Gaash in the hill-country of Joseph. The witnesses' patient placing of each captain builds, without their saying so, a map of a united realm; even the men "not further known" testify that the anointed king was served from every quarter of the land. The next verse turns back toward the Jordan valley and the fields of Bahurim.
31:
Two more captains are entered — "Abialbon the Arbathite, Azmaveth the Barhumite" — and the witnesses do the now-familiar work of correcting a garbled form and fixing a home. On the first name they are candid about the copyists. Charles Ellicott judges that "Abi-albon" is "in 1 Chronicles written Abiel, probably correctly," the extra syllable "having come in from Sha-albon-ite in the line below" — a scribe's eye, that is, straying to the next name. John Gill and Keil and Delitzsch concur that the man "is called Abiel" in the parallel, and place his home at "Beth-haarabah or Arabah," a town "in the desert of Judah" near the Jordan (Joshua 15:61).

The second name carries a heavier memory. Keil identifies Azmaveth as "of Bahurim" (2 Samuel 16:5), and Ellicott sharpens the form — "the Barhumite" should be "the Baharumite, i.e., of the Bahurim" mentioned in the story of David's flight. Bahurim is no neutral place-name: it was the village on the road from Jerusalem where Shimei the Benjamite ran along the hillside cursing the fleeing king and pelting him with stones, and where later David's spies hid in a well from Absalom's men. From that same hostile ground came one of David's thirty.

There is no debate here; the witnesses simply trace and correct. Yet the tracing keeps yielding the same quiet marvel. A captain of the guard hails from Bahurim, the town of Shimei's curse; another from the parched Arabah by the Jordan. The men who upheld David's throne came, some of them, from the very places bound up with his humiliation and flight — as if the roll of his glory were stitched together partly out of the scenes of his lowest days.

The witnesses do not press the point, but they lay the material for it plainly on the page. Every name they anchor to a town deepens the sense that David's kingdom was gathered from the whole varied land — its deserts and its cursing-places included — and that the LORD who "wrought a great victory" through such men delighted to raise his servants from unpromising soil. Abiel of the wilderness of Judah and Azmaveth of Shimei's Bahurim stand side by side in the honour-roll, their obscurity no bar to their being kept. The file moves on, in the next verse, to a name so tangled that even the witnesses can only confess the difficulty.
32:
Here the text grows knottiest, and the witnesses are unusually frank about it. The verse reads "Eliahba the Shaalbonite, of the sons of Jashen, Jonathan," and the trouble lies in that middle phrase. John Gill disposes of the first name easily — Eliahba was "of Shaalbon or Shaalbin, a city in the tribe of Dan," perhaps "the Silbonitis of Josephus." Keil and Delitzsch confirm the town, "possibly preserved in the present Selbit" (Joshua 19:42), but then admit that "the next two names" are so corrupt that "Bne-Jashen Jehonathan does not make any sense," and that the parallel in Chronicles — "Bne-hashem the Gizonite, Jonathan the son of Sage" — "is evidently the more correct of the two."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott labours at the same tangle. "The preposition of is not in the Hebrew, and should be omitted," he insists; the words "the sons of" may be no more than "an accidental repetition of the last three letters of the preceding word," or, if genuine, should be read as part of a proper name, "Jashen the Gizonite," leaving "Jonathan" as "a separate name." Whichever way the fragments are reassembled, the witnesses agree that a hero's name has been half-lost in the passage of the text.

There is no doctrinal debate here, only a shared honesty before a damaged verse. And that honesty is itself worth noting. The witnesses do not smooth over the difficulty or invent a tidy reading; they show their working, weigh Samuel against Chronicles, and confess where certainty fails. Reverence for the text, in their hands, means telling the truth about its wounds as well as its clarities.

Yet even a name eroded almost to illegibility is not thrown away. The Spirit thought this soldier worth enrolling, and the witnesses will not let him drop, however battered his name has come down. There is a kind of parable in it: a man remembered, yet only just — his town secure, his very name a matter of scholarly reconstruction. It is the mirror-image of the great names of the first three, and it says something about the whole roll. To be written among the mighty men of David is honour enough, even when time and copyists have worn the letters nearly smooth; the record holds, because the King whose it is does not forget. The file steadies again at the next verse with Shammah the Hararite and a hint that valour ran in families.
33:
The verse names "Shammah the Hararite, Ahiam the son of Sharar the Hararite," and the witnesses use it to recover a striking family connection. John Gill reads "Hararite" as "from the mountainous country," noting that the Targum, Arabic, and Syriac variously locate it, some even "from the mount of Olives." But the finer point belongs to Charles Ellicott, who observes that a "Shammah the son of Agee the Hararite has already been mentioned" among the first three (v.11), while here Chronicles reads "Jonathan the son of Shage the Hararite." Since "Shage is identical with Agee with a letter prefixed," Ellicott concludes "we should probably read Jonathan the son of Shammah the Hararite" — which means that "Jonathan, one of the thirty, was thus the son of one of the first three."

That is the verse's quiet treasure: valour running from father to son, the greatest rank and the ordinary rank joined in one family. Keil and Delitzsch, working the same textual ground, agree that the Chronicles form is the sounder and that "Shammah" here has probably crept in "from v.11," and they carry the correction on into the tangled names of the following verse. On the second man, Ahiam, the witnesses note only that his father's name appears as "Sharar" here and "Sacar" in Chronicles, and that even the spelling of "Hararite" "differs in the Hebrew here from the previous clause" — one more of the small variants that pepper the list.

There is no dispute of substance, only the patient reconstruction that turns a bare name into a relationship. And the relationship is worth savouring. If Jonathan the son of Shammah stands among the thirty while his father Shammah stands among the first three, then the roll preserves not merely individual heroes but a lineage of courage — the son honoured in his own right, though a step below the father. It is the same lesson the chapter has been teaching in its rankings: honour is real, degrees are real, and both are kept in the record.
