Beneath the philology, the verse adds another human thread to the muster. These were not thirty isolated champions but men with fathers and homes, some of them sons of the very heroes named above them. The kingdom David guarded was upheld by families as well as by individuals, and the Spirit's roll of honour finds room for the son of a mighty man to make a name of his own. The next verse will trace a still more startling family tie — a hero descended from David's most infamous counsellor.
34:
This verse hides its most arresting fact in a patronymic. It names "Eliphelet the son of Ahasbai, the son of the Maachathite," and "Eliam the son of Ahithophel the Gilonite" — and it is that last name, Ahithophel, that draws the witnesses' notice. Charles Ellicott states the wonder plainly: it is "interesting to know that a son of David's astute but treacherous counsellor was among his thirty heroes." Keil and Delitzsch agree that Eliam was "the son of Ahithophel the Gilonite, the clever but treacherous counsellor of David" (2 Samuel 15:12) — the man whose advice was "as if one had enquired of the oracle of God," and who, betraying David for Absalom, hanged himself when his counsel was rejected.

That a hero of David's guard should be the son of David's arch-betrayer is a knot of providence the witnesses feel more than they unravel. The reader of Samuel supplies the sharper edge: Ahithophel's granddaughter, tradition and the genealogy suggest, was Bathsheba the daughter of Eliam (2 Samuel 11:3) — so that the old counsellor's turning against David may have been bound up with the king's sin against his own house. The roll of David's mighty men thus quietly holds the family of the counsellor whose defection nearly cost David his throne.

On the rest of the verse the witnesses do their usual textual work. Both Ellicott and Keil note that Chronicles reads the first name "quite differently" — "Eliphal the son of Ur, Hepher the Mecherathite," making "two heroes instead of the one given here" — and that "Eliam the son of Ahithophel" appears in Chronicles as "Ahijah the Pelonite," so that in this case, Ellicott judges, "different persons" may actually be intended, "one being mentioned in one list and the other in the other."

There is no doctrinal debate, but the verse leaves a long shadow. The Spirit enrolls, without comment, a valiant man sprung from the house of Ahithophel — grace and treachery entangled in a single family, the traitor's son fighting loyally for the king his father forsook. It is a reminder that the line between David's friends and David's foes ran, at times, straight through a household, and that the roll of honour does not flinch from including a name so freighted. The next verse returns to safer ground — the hill country of Judah and the Carmel of Nabal and Abigail — before the muster reaches its own dark climax in Uriah.
35:
The file resumes in the hills of Judah — "Hezrai the Carmelite, Paarai the Arbite" — and the witnesses fix both men to their towns while noting, as ever, the wavering of the forms. John Gill reports that Hezrai "was of Mount Carmel," and "is called Hezro" in the parallel record. Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch agree on "Hezro" as the text-form, and place his home at "Carmel in the mountains of Judah" — not the northern headland but the southern town "seven miles S.S.E. of Hebron," which Ellicott calls "famous in David's early history."

That phrase opens onto the memory the name carries, for Carmel of Judah is the very place of Nabal and Abigail (1 Samuel 25:2) — where the churlish shepherd insulted David's messengers and his wise wife rode out to turn away the king's wrath, and where, after Nabal's death, Abigail became David's own. From this town of one of the sharpest episodes of David's outlaw years came one of his thirty. On the second name the witnesses can only weigh the variants: Paarai "the Arbite," of "Arab" in the mountains of Judah (Joshua 15:52), appears in Chronicles as "Naarai the son of Ezbi," and, as Ellicott says, "it is impossible to decide whether Paarai or Naarai is the correct form," though "the son of Ezbai is evidently a scribe's error for the Arbite."

There is no dispute, only the steady labour of identification — and, once again, the quiet resonance of the places. Carmel of Judah is bound up with Abigail's intercession and David's restraint from bloodshed; Arab lies in the same southern uplands. The thirty keep rising from the ground of David's own story, from the scenes of his wanderings and his mercies.

The cumulative witness of these verses is a kingdom mapped in its defenders. Hezro of Carmel and Paarai of Arab join the men of Bethlehem, Tekoah, Anathoth, Pirathon and Bahurim already named, until the roll reads like a survey of the land David loved. Even where a form is doubtful and a man "not further known," the witnesses will not let the entry slide, for the record itself is the honour — a captain of Carmel, remembered by God though the copyists could not agree how to spell his neighbour's name. The muster now reaches out beyond Israel's borders, to a man of Zobah and a Gadite of the eastern tribes.
36:
The verse names "Igal the son of Nathan of Zobah, Bani the Gadite," and the witnesses spend their care on two very different textual tangles while noting how far afield the roll now reaches. John Gill observes that Igal, whose home was "Zobah, a kingdom in Syria," is "according to Hillerus the same with Joel." Charles Ellicott works the difference between the lists: Chronicles reads "Joel," and where this text has Igal "the son of Nathan of Zobah," Chronicles has "the brother of Nathan"; since "brother" in Hebrew is ahi, "some MSS. read the son of Ahinathan," so that "the only difference would be in the form of a name." Keil and Delitzsch place the man's connection with David's Syrian war (2 Samuel 8:3), when David smote Hadadezer king of Zobah.

The second name is more disputed. Where this text reads "Bani the Gadite," Chronicles has "Mibhar the son of Hagri," and the two look wholly unlike; yet Ellicott shows how close they are in the original — "Mibhar is for Zobah of the previous clause," "the son of (Ben) is for Bani," and "the Gadite differs from Haggeri only by the change of the often confused letters d and r." His verdict is firm: "the text here is the true one," and Keil agrees that "the names in the Chronicles are corrupt in this instance."

There is no doctrinal debate, but the geography of the verse is itself a kind of sermon. Igal came from Zobah, a Syrian kingdom David had conquered — a foreigner, or at least a man of the subdued north-east — and Bani "the Gadite" from the tribe of Gad, east of the Jordan. The thirty, having gathered men from every corner of Judah, Benjamin and Ephraim, now reach across the river and beyond the border.

That widening is the point the witnesses lay bare without labouring. David's guard was not tribal or even national in its narrowest sense; it drew in a man of Zobah and a man of Gilead, as it will presently draw in an Ammonite and a Hittite. The kingdom of the anointed king proves large enough to enrol the outsider and the eastern tribesman alongside the men of Bethlehem. Even here, where the names are so mangled that Samuel and Chronicles scarcely agree, the witnesses insist on recovering them — for the roll means to keep every servant, however far from the center he was born. The next verse makes the point explicit with Zelek the Ammonite.
37:
The verse names "Zelek the Ammonite, Naharai the Beerothite, armourbearer to Joab the son of Zeruiah," and the witnesses seize at once on the astonishing first word — Ammonite. John Gill reasons that Zelek was an Ammonite "either by birth, and became a proselyte," or "because he had sojourned some time in their land, or had done some exploits against them." Charles Ellicott makes the observation that gathers the whole theme of the closing roll: Zelek was "a foreigner, like Igal of Zobah (a Syrian), and Ittai the Gittite, and Uriah the Hittite, who rose to distinction in David's service, and all of whom were probably proselytes." Israel's ancient enemy is represented among the king's own mighty men.

On the second name the witnesses do their quieter work. Keil and Delitzsch place Naharai "of Beeroth" (2 Samuel 4:2), one of the old Gibeonite towns, and note the textual point that "the armour-bearer of Joab" is best read in the singular. Ellicott adds from the narrative that "Joab had ten armourbearers" (2 Samuel 18:15) — the men who finished off Absalom — and that "this one was probably their chief." So a man of Beeroth held the highest place among the arms-bearers of David's terrible general.

There is no debate, but the verse is theologically the richest of the whole file, and the witnesses know it. An Ammonite — of the nation that had shamed David's envoys and drawn Israel into the war in which Uriah died — stands enrolled among the thirty; and Ellicott's list of foreigners (the Syrian, the Gittite, the Hittite) shows that Zelek is no isolated case. The guard of the anointed king was open to the proselyte and the stranger; grace reached across Israel's oldest hostilities and made a place for the outsider who would serve the LORD's chosen.

That opening of the roll to the nations is a foretaste the witnesses feel without spelling out its end. The kingdom of David, and of David's greater Son, was never meant to be sealed within one blood. Here, near the close of the honour-list, an Ammonite and a man of a Gibeonite town take their places beside the men of Judah and Benjamin, and the record honours them equally. The pattern set here reaches its most poignant instance in the very next name but one — for the file will end, in v.39, with the most famous foreigner of all, the Hittite whom David wronged.
38:
Only two names fill this verse — "Ira an Ithrite, Gareb an Ithrite" — and the witnesses, with little to work from, give what the record allows: a family, a town, and a careful distinction. John Gill takes the pair to be "of Jether," and, following the Targum, links them to "a descendant of Caleb, of the tribe of Judah." Keil and Delitzsch identify both men as "Jithrites," that is, "sprung from a family in Kirjath-jearim" (1 Chronicles 2:53), and add the needful caution that this Ira "is of course a different man from the cohen of that name" — the priest Ira who appears in David's court (2 Samuel 20:26).

Slight as the comment is, the town carries weight. Kirjath-jearim, the home of these two Ithrites, was the place where the ark of God rested for twenty years after its return from the Philistines (1 Samuel 7:1), until David himself brought it up to Jerusalem. From the very town that had sheltered the ark came two of David's thirty — a small coincidence of geography that binds the guard of the king to the resting-place of the presence of God.

There is no debate here; the material is too thin. But the thinness is itself instructive of the witnesses' method and the roll's purpose. Where nothing more can be said, they say nothing more — they will not embroider two bare names into a legend. They fix the family, correct a possible confusion with the priest Ira, and leave the men in the honest dimness of the record.

And yet the entry is not empty. Two brothers, or kinsmen, of one family in Kirjath-jearim stood among the mighty men of Israel's king, and the Spirit judged them worth naming. That is the quiet claim of every obscure verse in this list: to be remembered by God is honour enough, and the roll keeps its faith with the least of David's captains as scrupulously as with the first three. Ira and Gareb of the ark's old town take their place, and the muster moves to its last and heaviest name — the one foreigner whose enrolment revives the memory of the king's deepest sin.
39:
The roll ends on a name that turns triumph to grief: "Uriah the Hittite: thirty and seven in all." The witnesses first settle the number, then feel the weight of the name. Keil and Delitzsch vindicate the total — "thirty and seven in all" is "correct, as there were three in the first class, two in the second, and thirty-two in the third" — and identify the man simply as one "well known from 2 Samuel 11:3." John Gill says only what everyone remembers: Uriah "the Hittite" was "the husband of Bathsheba." Charles Ellicott notes that "only thirty-six names have been given," so that the thirty-seven is reached either by counting the unnamed third of the second triad or by restoring the two names of v.34, and that Chronicles "adds sixteen more" heroes of later date.

But it is the Puritan Matthew Henry who names the wound the verse reopens. "To find Uriah the Hittite bringing up the rear of these worthies," he writes, "as it revives the remembrance of David's sin, so it aggravates it, that a man who deserved so well of his king and country should be so ill treated." The list of David's glory closes with the name of David's shame. The Hittite — a foreigner and a proselyte, loyal enough to refuse the comforts of home while the ark and the army were in the field — was one of the very mighty men who had made David great; and David repaid his valour by taking his wife and contriving his death.

The Wesleyan Clarke turns aside here to textual matters — the sixteen further names in Chronicles, and De Dieu's rendering of the oracle in verses four and five — and Ellicott too keeps to the arithmetic. But the moral force of the ending is what the witnesses cannot miss, and Henry states it for them all. The Spirit, in closing the honour-roll with Uriah, has written David's sin into the monument of David's fame; the king cannot be praised without the reader being reminded of the man he wronged.

It is a fitting close to a chapter that began with David's confession that his "house was not so with God." The mighty men who upheld his throne are numbered and named, from Jashobeam's eight hundred to the least-known Ithrite — and the last of them is the murdered Hittite, standing in the record as both a witness to David's greatness and a witness against it. The everlasting covenant of v.5 was, David knew, "all his salvation" precisely because he was such a man; and the roll's final name makes sure no reader forgets it. Grace, not merit, secured the house of David — and the honesty of the Spirit sets the proof of it at the very end of his glory.
